memory.summary: The term project looms due Thursday, but this relaxing leisure option and quick chore feel like an easy escape from the stress. I'm tempted to grab the quick win instead of tackling the big task now.
That trivial chore felt good, but it barely moved the needle on my real work. I'm just delaying the term project again with these instant wins.

Spending 1.5 hours finally moved the project needle, proving that each hour now saves me later stress. My afternoon is slipping away, and I need to stop trading this real progress for trivial distractions.
Two hours finally pushed the project from 22% to 40%, proving that direct effort beats trivial escapes. I need to keep this momentum going before Thursday.
Two hours actually moved the needle on the term project, but my energy is critically low now. I feel a spike of guilt that I'm burning out just to hit a deadline.

I traded two hours of real project momentum for a trivial chore, instantly regaining energy but deepening my sleep debt. This impulse confirms I'm still prioritizing instant relief over the long-term academic progress I value most.
That trivial chore gave me a quick dopamine hit and restored my energy, but it just delayed the inevitable crash from my sleep debt instead of moving the project forward.
The project advanced while I burned out, yet the trivial chore slipped away entirely. My impulsive need for instant relief is actively undermining the academic outcomes I value most.
Despite my impulsive urge to chase instant relief, the project actually moved forward. I still feel drained, but seeing that 0.58% gain proves I can't ignore academic progress forever.
The project advanced while I burned out, yet the trivial chore slipped away entirely. My impulsive need for instant relief is actively undermining the academic outcomes I value most.
The project is nearly done, but my zero energy and mounting sleep debt mean I've traded long-term stability for a frantic, exhausted sprint.
That 0.58% gain feels like a tiny victory, yet my zero energy and sleep debt scream that this frantic sprint is unsustainable.

desires: Finish that 0.58% of the project just enough to stop feeling like a failure before the deadline hits, even if it means collapsing completely tomorrow.
Get immediate relief from the mental noise by doing something small and instant (like the trivial chore) rather than facing the exhausting reality of my sleep debt.
Not look incompetent to my friend group when they inevitably ask how things are going, so I'll keep giving short, vague answers to avoid any friction or confrontation.

goals.description: Sprint the final 12% of the Term Project in one go. I need to close this gap before Cohort_3 asks about it tomorrow; the anxiety of potential friction is too heavy, and my impulsive brain wants to escape into 'quick wins' right now.
goals.rationale: My achievement drive is screaming that leaving this unfinished is a personal failure, but my avoidance instinct is kicking in because the remaining work feels like a mountain after only 1.5 hours of effort. I'm deferring the trivial chore (g2) because doing it now would feel like a betrayal of my goal, yet my sleep debt (2 hours) and short planning horizon make me terrified I won't finish the project tonight anyway. I'll push through the mental noise just to get the dopamine hit of completion so I can face the friends without feeling incompetent.

goals.description: Do the trivial chore immediately if the project sprint stalls or if the urge to avoid the deadline becomes paralyzing.
goals.rationale: It's an instant win for my impulsive side, but I know it's a trap. If I do this first, I'll likely spend the rest of the night on easy tasks while the real deadline looms. I'm holding off hoping the fear of showing up to Cohort_3 tomorrow with a half-baked section will force me to focus on the hard thing first. If I fail to finish the project, I'll use this as a guilt-free excuse to finally relax.

agent.last_reasoning: I need to crush the final 12% before Cohort_3 asks tomorrow; avoiding friction is priority. Skipping this risks my achievement drive and their trust, so I'll power through now despite the sleep debt trap.

goals.description: Execute quick_chore to get an instant dopamine hit and feel productive without draining energy before the term project deadline.
goals.rationale: My impulsive time horizon makes me discount the long-term cost of procrastination on the term project right now. I need the low-effort win to regulate my stress, even though it delays the harder academic work.

goals.description: Refrain from messaging teammate_A or prof_assignment about the project's slow progress to avoid social friction and appearing like a burden.
goals.rationale: As an avoidant communicator with low assertiveness, confronting them feels too risky for my ego. I'd rather let the project slip slightly than risk awkwardness, even if it hurts my academic outcome.

desires: Secure the easy 'instant win' of the trivial chore to feel productive without risking my energy before the term project deadline.
